name: Shot Video Fidelity Binary
description: Binary evaluation of a shot video — judge applies the rubric to a SINGLE keyframe at a time. The audit tool aggregates across keyframes and adds the cross-frame motion checks (MOTION_PROGRESSED, STATIC_DRIFT, KEYFRAME_CONSISTENCY, DIRECTED_BEAT_REACHED) post-hoc by comparing first vs last frames. Per-keyframe score = (count YES) / 8 * 100.

questions.question: Is the primary subject named in the motion directive (or implied by the originating shot prompt) clearly visible and identifiable in this keyframe?
Does the primary subject match the original character description — age bracket, ethnicity, build, distinguishing features? Allow minor variation; fail only on identity-altering mismatches.
Does the visible setting match what the directive (or shot prompt) describes — interior/exterior, named location, atmospheric setting elements?
Does the composition broadly match what was directed — framing (wide / medium / close-up), camera angle, depth of field?

Is some recognizable trace of the directed action visible in this frame? E.g. if the directive says 'chrome flecks erupt from coat', do you see flecks/sparks/swirling debris around the character at this moment? Allow partial — the action need not be at climax in every frame.

Has the video NOT drifted to a totally unrelated subject? E.g. the directive says 'Johnathan striding' and the frame is a static extreme close-up of a chromed face — that's drift, fail. The frame should depict the directive's scene, not invent a different one.
Are there NO unintended characters in the frame — no extra people, no duplicates of the main character, no cat where no cat was specified?
Does the lighting and atmospheric mood match what was directed — time of day, colour temperature, key-light direction or quality when specified?
